purpose: Correlates WPI item-level series for diesel (HSD) and fertilisers (Urea, DAP) -- the domestic price points where imported mineral-fuel (HS27) and fertiliser (HS31) cost pressure actually lands -- against WPI and CPI headline/food inflation, to test the import-price-to-domestic-inflation transmission described in the cited literature.
source: MoSPI MCP connector: WPI dataset (base 2011-12), item-level HSD/Urea/Di-ammonium-phosphate plus headline; CPI dataset (base 2012), General/Food-and-Beverages/Oils-and-Fats sub-groups, All-India Combined
method: Annual April-anchor snapshot (the April index value of each calendar year) for WPI (Apr2012-Apr2026) and CPI (Apr2013-Apr2025), chosen to match this repo's existing FY/annual chart convention rather than a full monthly series.

cpi_oils_and_fats_yoy.note: Full monthly CPI oils-and-fats year-on-year inflation swung from this trough to this peak -- the sharpest reversal of any major CPI sub-group in the 2012-2026 window -- consistent with near 1:1 global-to-domestic pass-through for edible oils per Mishra and Roy (2011-12).

cited_literature.author: James D. Hamilton
cited_literature.title: Import Prices and Inflation
cited_literature.venue: International Journal of Central Banking, March 2012
cited_literature.key_point: Pass-through from import prices to domestic inflation is not a fixed number -- it depends on what caused the import price to change in the first place; raw historical correlation answers a conditional-forecasting question, not a causal one.

cited_literature.author: Rudrani Bhattacharya and Abhijit Sen Gupta
cited_literature.title: Food Inflation in India: Causes and Consequences
cited_literature.key_point: SVAR estimates: a 10% rise in fuel inflation raises food inflation ~1% within the same month (transitory); a 10% rise in agricultural-wage inflation raises food inflation 1.6% immediately, rising to 2.4% within 4 months (post-2008: up to 5.5%). Edible oil inflation shows near-1:1 pass-through from global edible-oil prices; cereals show almost none, insulated by trade/procurement policy.

cited_literature.author: Prachi Mishra and Devesh Roy
cited_literature.title: Explaining Inflation in India: The Role of Food Prices
cited_literature.key_point: Degree of global-to-domestic pass-through tracks tradability: edible oils (heavily imported) move almost one-for-one with world prices; rice, wheat, sugar (protected by export bans/procurement) barely move with them.

cited_literature.author: Dr. Ashique Elahi and Dr. Bijoy Kr. Nath
cited_literature.title: Inflationary Trends and Their Influence on India's Economic Stability
cited_literature.venue: Journal of Emerging Technologies and Innovative Research (JETIR), April 2025
cited_literature.key_point: Names global crude oil prices feeding into domestic fuel/diesel costs as 'a classic example of imported inflation in India'; cites RBI finding that inflation above 6% starts to drag on GDP growth.

cited_literature.author: Yogeshwaran A.
cited_literature.title: An Analysis of Indian Imports and Their Impact on GDP and the Economy
cited_literature.key_point: Crude petroleum is 23.4% of India's total import bill by value (the single largest line item); fertiliser categories (mixed mineral/chemical, nitrogenous) add roughly another 2%.
